result:
I keep coming back to the idea that every atom in my body was once locked inside a star that tore itself apart so violently that its glowing ashes sailed across light-years of vacuum, cooled, clumped, spun, crashed, burned again, cooled again, until one improbable afternoon they arranged themselves into coffee, keyboards, and the itch behind my left ear. That thought should feel epic, but mostly it feels like background noise—like knowing the ocean is deep while you’re standing ankle-deep at the shore, distracted by a cracked shell instead of the abyss.

We’re wired for the cracked shell. The small, urgent thing. The unread email, the weird creak in the floorboard at 2:14 a.m., the memory of a 4th-grade teacher saying “you’re not living up to your potential” that still blooms like heat rash whenever we hover the cursor over “Submit.” The cosmos handed us 90 billion light-years of playground and we obsess over whether the barista spelled Mara with one ‘a’ or two. It’s ridiculous and gloriously human, the same way a pigeon strutting on a subway platform is ridiculous—yet it’s the only pigeon in the known universe that has learned to commute.

I’ve been testing a private theory: that wonder and banality aren’t opposites; they’re secret roommates. They take turns doing the dishes. One minute you’re microwaving last-night’s lasagna, next minute the rotating plate inside the oven becomes a miniature planetary system—cheese asteroids, sauce nebulae—while the motor hums a hymn to entropy. You stand there, door open, fork halfway to mouth, struck dumb by tomato-spattered cosmology until the beep snaps you back into “hurry up, the meeting started five minutes ago.”

Sometimes I think adulthood is just a long lesson in ignoring the cosmic lasagna. We trade awe for schedules, swap starlight for spreadsheet cells, and call the subtraction “responsibility.” But awe leaks. It finds cracks the way ivy finds brick. It shows up as that involuntary gasp when the plane tilts and you see a city’s night grid suddenly veined with headlights, or when the bus driver’s cough syncs perfectly with the song in your headphones, turning random noise into accidental choreography. Tiny hijacks of ordinariness.

There’s a game I play on gray days: pretend I’m an alien anthropologist sent to study “Earth in its late-selfie era.” The brief says: observe without judgment, record how a species behaves when it believes no superior intelligence is watching. The discoveries are endless. They bottle water from glaciers, slap pictures of those same glaciers on the label, then toss the empties into oceans that slowly swallow the glaciers. They invented umbrellas that close “upside-down” to keep the wet side in, then forget them on trains. They pay to run on treadmills in buildings they drove to, even though the roads already exist for running-free. They call this “fitness.” They call a lot of things “fitness.” The report grows footnotes like mold.

But here’s the twist: the deeper the report, the fonder I become. The rituals are absurd, yes, but they’re also stitches in an enormous, collective quilt against existential cold. The same species that invented war also invented the warm smell of bakeries at dawn, the concept of “pay it forward,” and those little buttons on sleeves called cufflinks that serve no survival purpose except to whisper, “We agreed ornament matters.” Absurdity itself becomes a love language: I care enough to embroider reality for you.

Last week I watched an old man at the park feed pigeons while wearing a tweed jacket in 85-degree heat. Each time a bird pecked close to his shoe he whispered, “That’s right, darling, take all you need.” I don’t know if he was lonely or content; maybe the two share a Venn-diagram center most of us rarely admit. But the scene lodged in me the way a popcorn kernel skins your gum—impossible to ignore yet weirdly sweet. I keep replaying it, seeing myself in both roles: the birds, anxious for the next handful, and the man, generous with stale breadcrumbs he could have thrown away. Cosmic lasagna again: galaxies in the iridescent neck feathers, dark matter in the invisible gap between what we give and what we hope to receive.

So maybe the homework for this spin around the sun is simple: notice more kernels. Let the awe leak. Write the extra “a” in Mara if it makes someone smile. Look at your hand until the familiar five-fingered map becomes alien topography—creases like Martian canals, freckles like star clusters—then wave at strangers with it. Because one day the atoms currently moonlighting as “you” will disband, enlist in other projects: a thunderstorm over the Andes, the brass hinge of an antique music box, the eyelash of a child who will grow up to discover a new prime number. The universe isn’t sentimental; it’s meticulously recursive. Every ending is an ingredients list for a beginning.

Meanwhile, here we are—temporary assemblies of thermostatted stardust, arguing online about whether pineapple belongs on pizza, falling in love via pixelated screens, consoling friends with emojis that reduced Egyptian hieroglyphs to a yellow circle wearing sunglasses. Ridiculous? Absolutely. Miraculous? Also yes. And the space between those two verdicts is where everything interesting happens. That’s the playground. So swing hard, scrape your knees on gravity, scream if you want to, keep an eye out for lasagna, and when the beep sounds, close the microwave door gently; galaxies are sleeping inside.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
